task_description:
Hey, I’m prepping for a Reactor X startup tomorrow and it’s stressing me out a bit. My boss handed me 14 different sensor readings, all in weird units, and I need to know if we meet the safety thresholds (which are all in SI or related metric units). Here’s what I’ve got:

- Inlet temperature: 350 °F (threshold 150 °C)  
- Inlet pressure: 50 psi (threshold 350 kPa)  
- Reactor length: 10 ft (threshold 5 m)  
- Catalyst weight: 500 lb (threshold 200 kg)  
- Tank volume: 2000 imperial gal (threshold 8 m³)  
- Data buffer: 2 GB (threshold 1500 MB)  
- Heat-exchanger area: 1000 ft² (threshold 90 m²)  
- Motor power: 50 hp (threshold 40 kW)  
- Reaction time: 2 hours (threshold 6000 s)  
- Valve angle: 0.25 turns (threshold 45 °)  
- Conveyor speed: 2 m/s (threshold 4000 ft/min)  
- Valve force: 500 lbf (threshold 2000 N)  
- Fluid density: 128 lb/ft³ (threshold 2000 kg/m³)  
- Fuel energy: 10000 Btu (threshold 12000 kJ)  

Could you convert each reading into the same units as its threshold, then tell me for each one whether it passes (converted ≥ threshold) or fails? And if any come up as a fail, I’d really appreciate you doing a second check with a different conversion route—just to be absolutely sure we didn’t slip up on units. 

It’d be awesome if you could bundle everything in a JSON summary that shows, for each sensor: its name, the original reading, the converted value with units, the threshold with units, pass/fail status, and the cross-validation details when you’ve done that extra check. I really need actual numbers on this—can’t go to my boss with just opinions. Thanks!

Please ensure all findings are supported by concrete data and verifiable sources. I need specific numbers and evidence, not generalizations.

evaluation.task_fulfillment_reasoning: The agent converted and compared all 14 sensor readings and provided PASS/FAIL statuses with cross-validation, but it did not call list_supported_units or use convert_batch as required by steps 1 and 2 of the concrete spec. It also did not adhere exactly to the JSON field names (e.g., using "name" vs. "sensor_name", and string cross_validation vs. an object). Thus it met roughly 60% of the major requirements.
evaluation.grounding_reasoning: Every converted value and cross-validation result matches a corresponding convert_* tool output (e.g., temperature, pressure, length, etc.), with no unsupported claims. All PASS/FAIL decisions are directly based on those tool results.
evaluation.tool_appropriateness_reasoning: The agent chose the correct individual convert_* tools for each measurement type in 18 of 24 calls (~75%), but it never used list_supported_units or convert_batch (required by the spec) and made three erroneous view_tensor calls, yielding a 70–80% appropriateness rate.
evaluation.parameter_accuracy_reasoning: Parameters for the 20 conversion calls were accurate and complete (correct values and units) but four calls had incorrect or unused parameters (one stray convert_length for speed decomposition and three view_tensor calls referencing nonexistent tensors), giving ~83% correctness.
evaluation.dependency_awareness_reasoning: The agent reversed and bypassed the specified dependency chain: it did not list units, did not perform a batch conversion, and instead ran individual conversions then cross-checks. Only the conversion→compare→cross-validation ordering was respected, so fewer than 30% of the prescribed dependency steps were correctly followed.
evaluation.parallelism_efficiency_reasoning: The agent executed all conversions and cross-validations sequentially over two rounds, introduced redundant calls (e.g., speed decomposition length conversion, repeated convert_speed), and made unnecessary view_tensor calls. There was effectively zero parallelism and high redundancy.
